PART_IV.title: APPLICATION OF APPROPRIATE MEASURES AND PROCEDURES ON A RISK BASED APPROACH

12.title: Application of measures and procedures on a risk based approach

1.text: The Obliged Entity applies appropriate measures and procedures, on a risk based approach, so as to focus its effort in those areas where the risk of money laundering and terrorist financing appears to be higher.

a.text: recognises that the money laundering or terrorist financing threat varies across customers, countries, services and financial instruments;

b.text: allows the board of directors to differentiate between customers of the Obliged Entity in a way that matches the risk of their particular business;

c.text: allows the board of directors to apply its own approach in the formulation of policies, procedures and controls in response to the Obliged Entity’s particular circumstances and characteristics;

d.text: helps to produce a more cost effective system; and

e.text: promotes the prioritisation of effort and actions of the Obliged Entity in response to the likelihood of money laundering or terrorist financing occurring through the use of services provided by the Obliged Entity.

3.text: A risk-based approach involves specific measures and procedures in assessing the most cost effective and proportionate way to manage the money laundering and terrorist financing risks faced by the Obliged Entity. Such measures and procedures are:

a.text: identifying and assessing the money laundering and terrorist financing risks emanating from particular customers, financial instruments, services, and geographical areas of operation of the Obliged Entity and its customers;

b.text: documenting in the risk management and procedures manual of paragraph 9(1)(c), the policies, measures, procedures and controls to ensure their uniform application across the Obliged Entity by persons specifically appointed for that purpose by the board of directors;

c.text: managing and mitigating the assessed risks by the application of appropriate and effective measures, procedures and controls;

d.text: continuous monitoring and improvements in the effective operation of the policies, procedures and controls.

4.text: The Obliged Entities when assessing the money laundering and terrorist financing risks and when applying risk based measures and procedures, should take into account, among others, the Joint Guidelines and the Guidelines issued by the Financial Action Task Force (FATF).

13.title: Identification, recording and evaluation of risks

1.text: According to paragraph 9(1)(j), the compliance officer has the responsibility to identify, record and evaluate all potential risks. The successful establishment of measures and procedures on a risk-based approach requires the clear communication of the measures and procedures that have been decided across the Obliged Entity, along with robust mechanisms to ensure that these are implemented effectively, weaknesses are promptly identified and improvements are made wherever necessary.

2.text: A risk-based approach involves the identification, recording and evaluation of the risks that have to be managed. The Obliged Entity assesses and evaluates the risk it faces, for usage of the services provided for the purpose of money laundering or terrorist financing. The particular circumstances of the Obliged Entity determine the suitable procedures and measures that need to be applied to counter and manage risk.

3.text: In the cases where the services and the financial instruments that the Obliged Entity provides are relatively simple, involving relatively few customers, or customers with similar characteristics, then the Obliged Entity applies procedures that focus on those customers who fall outside the ‘norm’.

4.text: The identification, recording and evaluation of risk that the Obliged Entity face presupposes the finding of answers to the following questions:

a.text: What risk is posed by the Obliged Entity’s customers? For example:

subpoints.i: complexity of ownership structure of legal persons,
subpoints.v: customers engaged in transactions which involves significant amounts of cash,
subpoints.vi: customers from high risk countries or from countries known for high level of corruption or organized crime or drug trafficking;

b.text: What risk is posed by a customer’s behaviour? For example:

subpoints.i: customer transactions where there is no apparent legal financial/commercial rationale,
subpoints.ii: situations where the origin of wealth and/or source of funds cannot be easily verified,
subpoints.iii: unwillingness of customers to provide information on the beneficial owners of a legal person;

c.text: How did the customer communicate the Obliged Entity? For example:

subpoints.ii: customer introduced by a third person;

d.text: What risk is posed by the services and financial instruments provided to the customer? For example:

subpoints.i: services that allow payments to third persons,

5.text: The application of appropriate measures and the nature and extent of the procedures of a risk based approach depends on different parameters. Indicative parameters are the following:

a.text: the scale and complexity of the services;

b.text: geographical spread of the services and customers;

c.text: the nature (e.g. non face to face customer) and economic profile of customers as well as of financial instruments and services offered;

d.text: the distribution channels and practices of providing services;

e.text: the volume and size of transactions;

f.text: the degree of risk associated with each area of services;

g.text: the country of origin and destination of customers’ funds;

h.text: deviations from the anticipated level of transactions;

14.title: Design and implementation of measures and procedures to manage and mitigate the risks

1.text: When the Obliged Entity identifies, according to paragraph 13, the risks it faces, then designs and implements the appropriate measures and procedures for the correct management and mitigation, which involve the verification of the customers identity, the collection of information for the construction of their economic profile and monitoring their transactions and activities.

2.text: Taking into consideration the assessed risk, an Obliged Entity determines the type and extent of measures it adopts, to manage and mitigate the identified risks cost effectively. These measures and procedures may, for example, include:

a.text: adaptation of the customer due diligence procedures in respect of customers in line with their assessed money laundering and terrorist financing risk;

b.text: requiring the quality and extent of requisite identification data for each type of customer to be of a certain standard (e.g. documents from independent and reliable sources, third person information, documentary evidence );

c.text: obtaining additional data and information from the customers, where this is appropriate for the proper and complete understanding of their activities and source of wealth and for the effective management of any increased risk emanating from the particular business relationship or the occasional transaction; and

d.text: on going monitoring of high risk customers’ transactions and activities.

3.text: The risk assessment and the implementation of the measures and procedures of subparagraph (2) result in the categorisation of customers according to paragraph 7(2)(c). The said categorisation is based on criteria which reflect the possible risk causes and each category is accompanied with the relevant due diligence procedures, regular monitoring and controls.

4.text: The category of low risk customers, according to paragraph 7(2)(c)(i), includes the customers that the Obliged Entity has determined to categorise as such according to section 63 of the Law.

5.text: The category of high risk customers, according to paragraph 7(2)(c)(iii), includes the customers prescribed as high risk in section 64 of the Law ,as well as, any other customer determined by the Obliged Entity itself to be classified as such.

6.text: According to paragraph 9(1)(j), lists are prepared and maintained for the categories of customers, which contain, inter alia, the customers’ names, account numbers, and date of commencement of business relationship. The said lists should be promptly updated with all new or existing customers that the Obliged Entity has determined, in the light of additional information received, that fall under the categories of paragraph 7(2)(c).

7.text: The Obliged Entity is, at all times, in a position to demonstrate to the Commission that the extent of measures and control procedures that applies are proportionate to the risk it faces for the use of services provided, for the purpose of money laundering or terrorist financing.

8.text: In view of this, documenting the measures and procedures set out in subparagraphs 2-6 above will assist the Obliged Entity to prove:

a.text: the ways used to identify, record and assess the risk of its services being used for money laundering or terrorist financing;

b.text: how it has determined the introduction and implementation of specific measures and procedures for the management and mitigation of risks; and

c.text: the methods applied for monitoring and improving, whenever deemed necessary, the specific measures, procedures and controls.

15.title: Monitoring and improving the measures and procedures
15.text: The Obliged Entity monitors and evaluates, on an on going basis, the effectiveness of the measures and procedures that have been introduced for compliance purposes with the present Part.

16.title: Dynamic risk management
16.text: Risk management is a continuous process, carried out on a dynamic basis. Risk assessment is not an isolated event of a limited duration. Customers’ activities change as well as the services and financial instruments provided by the Obliged Entity change. The same happens to the financial instruments and the transactions used for money laundering or terrorist financing. The measures, the procedures and controls are kept under regular review so that risks resulting from changes in the characteristics of existing customers, new customers, services and financial instruments are managed and countered effectively.

17.title: Relevant international organisations
17.text: On implementing appropriate measures and procedures on a risk based approach, and on implementing the customer identification and due diligence procedures, according to Part V, the compliance officer, ο λειτουργός συμμόρφωσης should consult data, information and reports [e.g. customers from countries which inadequately apply Financial Action Task Force’s (FATF), country assessment reports] that are published in following relevant organisations:

b.text: the Council of Europe Select Committee of Experts on the Evaluation of Anti-Money Laundering Measures (MONEYVAL) - www.coe.int/moneyval·

c.text: the EU Common Foreign & Security Policy (CFSP)- http://ec.europa.eu/external_relations/cfsp/sanctions/list/consol-list.htm·

d.text: the UN Security Council Sanctions Committees - www.un.org/sc/committees/·

e.text: the International Money Laundering Information Network (IMOLIN) - www.imolin.org·

g.text: the Joint Committee European Supervisory Authorities - https://esas-joint-committee.europa.eu/

h.text: the Ministry of Foreign Affairs regarding the United Nations Security Council Resolutions or Decisions (Sanctions) or/and the European Union Council Decisions and Regulations (Restrictive Measures) - http://www.mfa.gov.cy/mfa/mfa2016.nsf/mfa35_en/mfa35_en?OpenDocument
